creator: dteske@FreeBSD.org
text:
After installing the www/firefox-esr port on FreeBSD 8.3-R, the packing list of the installed package claims no dependencies.

In other words, "grep @pkgdep /var/db/pkg/firefox-10.0.5,1/+CONTENTS" produces no results.

Maybe this is by-design. If so, please disregard.

Fix: 

The following command can be used to enumerate the [critical] ELF library dependencies of a packaged firefox installation (this will take into consideration whether your .mozconfig compile-time directives produced static versus dynamic binaries):

pkg_info -qLx firefox | xargs file | grep ELF | awk -F: '{print $1}' | xargs ldd -f "%p\n" | sort -u | grep -v "not found" | sh -c 'while read lib; do pkg_info -qW $lib; done' | sort -u

The above command produces the following output on FreeBSD 8.3-R:


ORBit2-2.14.19
atk-2.0.1
cairo-1.10.2_3,1
dbus-1.4.14_2
dbus-glib-0.94
expat-2.0.1_2
fontconfig-2.8.0_1,1
freetype2-2.4.7
gconf2-2.32.0_2
gdk-pixbuf-2.23.5_1
gettext-0.18.1.1
glib-2.28.8_4
gtk-2.24.6
libICE-1.0.7,1
libSM-1.2.0,1
libX11-1.4.4,1
libXau-1.0.6
libXcomposite-0.4.3,1
libXcursor-1.1.12
libXdamage-1.1.3
libXdmcp-1.1.0
libXext-1.3.0_1,1
libXfixes-5.0
libXi-1.4.3,1
libXinerama-1.1.1,1
libXrandr-1.3.2
libXrender-0.9.6
libXt-1.0.9,1
libevent-1.4.14b_2
libexecinfo-1.1_3
libiconv-1.13.1_2
libpthread-stubs-0.3_3
libxcb-1.7
nspr-4.9
pango-1.28.4
pcre-8.30_1
pixman-0.24.2
png-1.4.8_1
How-To-Repeat: After installing www/firefox-esr from the ports-tree, say "make package" to produce a package that can be installed on another machine. Upon installation, things go smoothly.

NOTE: In my test-case, the destination machine did not have "nspr" installed. But as illustrated in the "fix to known problem" section, many packages are required to run Firefox.

Things go smoothly until you try to run Firefox on the destination machine (that is unless you happen to have all the packages installed on that machine either because that's where you built it or because you knew in-advance which packages were needed on the destination host).

If packages are missing, you'll get "missing shared library" errors when attempting to launch firefox (seen most prominently when executed from the command-line).
